content:
The browser wasn’t blocking my server. It was protecting me.

8 min read
3 hours ago

--

The first time I hit a CORS error, I did what everyone does.
I copied the exact red text into Google. I found a Stack Overflow answer with thousands of upvotes. I pasted Access-Control-Allow-Origin: * into my Express server. It worked. I moved on.
Read Free for non-members.
I did that for three years. Every single time. I never once stopped to ask what the browser was actually checking.
Then a junior dev asked me to explain it in a meeting. Out loud. And I froze. I had “fixed” this error a hundred times and I had no idea what I was talking about.
So I finally sat down and learned it. Here’s everything I wish I’d known on day one.
The Setup You’ve Probably Lived Through
You’re calling your own API from your own frontend. You wrote both sides. You know the data is safe.
The browser does not care.
It throws a red error about Access-Control-Allow-Origin not being present. And you sit there confused, because the exact same request worked fine in Postman five seconds ago.
Most tutorials make this moment worse. They hand you the fix before the idea. Add this header. Set this flag. Done.
